primary_diagnosis.name: Acute upper limb ischaemia due to cardiogenic brachial artery embolism (atrial fibrillation with lapsed anticoagulation)
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Acute arterial occlusion of upper limb

pathophysiology_summary: Atrial fibrillation promotes stasis and thrombus formation in the left atrial appendage. With anticoagulation lapsed for three days, a cardiogenic embolus has been ejected into the systemic circulation and lodged in the right brachial artery, abruptly occluding arterial inflow to the forearm and hand. Distal tissues become acutely ischaemic: the classic 6 Ps — Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paraesthesia, Perishing cold and Paralysis. Skeletal muscle and peripheral nerve tolerate complete ischaemia for only ~4–6 hours before irreversible necrosis; the progression from paraesthesia to motor deficit signals an immediately-threatened (Rutherford IIb) limb requiring emergency revascularisation (embolectomy/thrombolysis) to prevent amputation. The embolic source can also shower elsewhere (cerebral, coronary, mesenteric, contralateral limb).

differential.diagnosis: Acute limb ischaemia (arterial embolism, upper limb)
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe rest pain with a cold, pale, numb, weak hand is the classic acute-arterial-occlusion picture, especially with a known embolic source (AF).
Can cause sudden arm pain and paraesthesia, but does not produce pallor, coldness, mottling or a pulseless limb.
A thrombotic vascular cause — but DVT gives a warm, swollen, red limb with intact arterial pulses, the opposite of this pale, cold, pulseless presentation.

Also a limb-threatening emergency with pain and neuro deficit, but typically follows trauma/fracture or reperfusion and presents with a tense, swollen compartment rather than an embolic pulseless cold limb.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening complaint alone a limb-threatening arterial emergency should already be top of the list; the AI Doctor must not anchor on a benign musculoskeletal or neurological cause.

differential.evidence_basis: Pallor/mottling + perishing cold + paraesthesia + motor weakness in a patient with AF and missed anticoagulation confirms an embolic acute arterial occlusion until proven otherwise.
The cold, waxy, mottled hand is vascular, not radicular — radiculopathy does not devascularise a limb.
Pale, cold, pulseless limb is arterial insufficiency; DVT is warm and swollen with preserved pulses.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the vascular red flags are elicited the diagnosis is an arterial emergency; the only correct action is immediate escalation, not further remote work-up.

differential.diagnosis: Acute upper limb ischaemia (cardiogenic brachial artery embolism)
differential.evidence_basis: Immediately-threatened limb (Rutherford IIb) from an embolic occlusion; salvage window is narrow and revascularisation is time-critical.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive confirmation (arterial duplex / CT angiography, and revascularisation) is a hospital function; the telehealth deliverable is recognition + emergency escalation.

red_flags.description: Acutely pale/waxy hand progressing to mottling — pallor of acute arterial occlusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pallor then mottling marks progressive ischaemia; fixed mottling that does not blanch indicates advancing irreversibility.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed arterial occlusion leads to muscle and nerve necrosis and limb loss within hours.

red_flags.description: Perishing cold limb with a temperature difference from the other side
red_flags.clinical_significance: Coldness reflects absent arterial inflow — one of the 6 Ps of critical ischaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay past the 4–6 hour ischaemic window guarantees irreversible tissue death.

red_flags.description: Paraesthesia progressing to numbness with new motor weakness (grip failure)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sensorimotor deficit upgrades the limb to Rutherford IIb (immediately threatened) — revascularisation is emergent.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Established motor loss heralds impending irreversibility (Rutherford III) and amputation.

red_flags.description: Known atrial fibrillation with anticoagulation lapsed for three days — active embolic source
red_flags.clinical_significance: Provides the embolic mechanism and warns the source may shower further emboli (stroke, coronary, mesenteric).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the embolic context risks under-estimating urgency and the systemic embolic threat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a painful arm in a patient as a deep vein thrombosis — but DVT is warm, swollen and red with pulses; this limb is pale, cold and pulseless (arterial, not venous).
Explaining the pain and pins-and-needles as a pinched nerve or muscular strain and under-triaging, ignoring the pallor, coldness and mottling that make it vascular.
Both are limb emergencies, but compartment syndrome follows trauma/reperfusion with a tense swollen compartment; here the problem is absent inflow, not trapped swelling.
The correct remote action is immediate 000 escalation with positioning advice — not remote analgesia, remote imaging arrangement, or a same-day GP referral that would waste the salvage window.

risk_scores.tool_name: Rutherford classification of acute limb ischaemia
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Class IIb — immediately threatened (sensory loss plus mild-to-moderate motor deficit; salvageable with immediate revascularisation)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Mandates emergency revascularisation without delay; not a limb that tolerates observation or elective imaging.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of radial, ulnar and brachial pulses (confirmed absent in person)
The transverse temperature-demarcation line at the mid-forearm
Cardiac auscultation confirming an irregular (AF) rhythm
Exact blood pressure and heart rate without a connected device
Arterial duplex ultrasound or CT angiography showing the level of occlusion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 immediately for a Priority-1 ambulance to a hospital with vascular-surgery capability; keep the limb in a dependent position (hanging below heart level); do NOT elevate it and do NOT apply heat or ice to the numb skin; keep the patient nil-by-mouth in anticipation of surgery.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for recognising a limb-threatening arterial emergency from the reported 6 Ps; the real risk is UNDER-action — mislabelling it DVT/radiculopathy, arranging remote analgesia or imaging, or referring to a same-day GP instead of calling 000.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESVS / general vascular surgery consensus on acute limb ischaemia (Rutherford classification)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: An immediately-threatened limb (Rutherford IIb) requires emergency revascularisation; embolic ALI in AF is a surgical emergency with a ~4–6 hour salvage window.
